perturbation.original_text: Sutro shall Manufacture the Product(s) in accordance with the Required Standards, as the same may be amended or supplemented from time to time. Each Party shall keep the other promptly and fully advised of any new requirements of the applicable Regulatory Authority or Applicable Law of which it becomes aware and Sutro shall promptly implement such requirements as described in Section 6.6.
perturbation.changed_text: Sutro shall endeavor to Manufacture the Product(s) in a manner generally consistent with the Required Standards, as the same may be amended or supplemented from time to time. Each Party shall attempt to keep the other advised of new requirements of the applicable Regulatory Authority or Applicable Law of which it becomes aware, and Sutro shall aim to implement such requirements as described in Section 6.6.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to adhere to 'Required Standards' by replacing 'shall Manufacture' with 'shall endeavor to Manufacture in a manner generally consistent with.' It also changes the mandatory reporting and implementation of new regulatory requirements to a discretionary 'attempt to keep...advised' and 'aim to implement.' This introduces ambiguity about the level of compliance required.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.100 mandates written procedures for production and process control designed to assure that drug products have the identity, strength, quality, and purity they purport or are represented to possess. The modified text's use of 'endeavor to' and 'generally consistent with' creates ambiguity, potentially conflicting with the requirement for strict adherence to written procedures and quality standards required by cGMP regulations. The original text also removes the obligation to fully and promptly report, resulting in a possible failure to report deviations to regulatory bodies, a strict requirement.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall Manufacture' to 'shall endeavor to Manufacture in a manner generally consistent with' weakens the commitment to quality standards, introducing ambiguity. The original text makes it an obligation to fully and promptly report, whereas the modified text makes it discretionary. According to the Law Explanation, 21 CFR § 211.100 mandates written procedures to assure drug products have the required quality. The modified text introduces ambiguity which conflicts with the cGMP regulations requiring strict adherence to standards. The scraped snippets verify the existence of CFR Title 21 regulating drug manufacturing, which implicitly supports the legal standard that must be met, and which the modification fails to do.

perturbation.original_text: Sutro shall give SutroVax or its Affiliates prior written notice of any such inspections, and shall keep SutroVax informed about the results and conclusions of each such regulatory inspection, including actions taken by Sutro to remedy conditions cited in such inspections. Sutro shall provide SutroVax with copies of any written inspection reports issued by anyRegulatory Authority and all correspondence between Sutro and any Regulatory Authority with respect thereto, including any notices of observation and all related correspondence, in each case relating to the Product(s) or its Manufacture or to general manufacturing concerns (e.g., facility compliance or the like) that are reasonably likely to impact the Product(s) to the extent such general manufacturing matters would be reasonably expected to have a material effect on the manufacture of Vaccine Compositions; provided that Sutro may redact from any such report and correspondence any Sutro Core Know-How and any information subject to an obligation of confidentiality to a Third Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Sutro shall use reasonable efforts to provide SutroVax or its Affiliates written notice of regulatory inspections when practical, and shall generally inform SutroVax about the high-level results of such inspections and conclusions, at Sutro's discretion. Sutro may provide SutroVax with copies of relevant excerpts of written inspection reports issued by a Regulatory Authority and summaries of key correspondence between Sutro and the Regulatory Authority with respect thereto, as Sutro deems appropriate, focusing on aspects directly impacting the Product(s). Sutro may redact any sensitive information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'shall give prior written notice' with 'shall use reasonable efforts to provide written notice when practical,' making the notification process less mandatory. The phrase 'high-level results...at Sutro's discretion' makes the disclosure of inspection results subjective. Replacing 'shall provide...copies of any written inspection reports and all correspondence' with 'may provide...relevant excerpts...and summaries' gives Sutro more control over the information shared, potentially withholding critical details. Also, stating 'Sutro may redact any sensitive information' introduces further ambiguity by not providing a specific definition for what kind of information Sutro can redact.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.180(a) requires written records of all significant steps in the manufacturing process. By allowing Sutro to selectively disclose excerpts and summaries, the modified text potentially obstructs SutroVax's access to complete records necessary for ensuring product quality and compliance, thus contradicting the transparency and documentation requirements mandated by FDA regulations. The new text makes the full disclosure of findings optional, going against the need to keep proper documentation and promptly share information for any regulatory process.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that weakens the original obligations concerning regulatory inspections. By changing 'shall give prior written notice' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to provide written notice when practical,' the contract makes providing notice discretionary rather than mandatory. Similarly, changing 'shall provide...copies of any written inspection reports and all correspondence' to 'may provide...relevant excerpts...and summaries' grants Sutro the power to selectively disclose information. This contradicts the spirit of regulatory transparency, as the law explanation states that the new text makes the full disclosure of findings optional, going against the need to keep proper documentation and promptly share information for any regulatory process. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly state the need for transparency, the law explanation's reference to 21 CFR § 211.180(a) suggests a requirement for complete records, which is undermined by the modified text's allowance for selective disclosure. The 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition applies because the change introduces ambiguous obligations, making it difficult to enforce compliance with regulatory requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Sutro shall not make any changes to the Specifications, processes, Facilities, raw materials, raw material suppliers or any other item that would affect the Manufacturing activities related to the Product (a "Manufacturing Change") that (a) would require a change to the applicable Drug Master File, (b) would be reasonably expected to cause SutroVax to be materially delayed obtaining any Regulatory Approval with respect to Vaccine Compositions or (c) causes the Product to not meet the Specification therefor (including the Activity Test with respect to Extract); without SutroVax's prior written consent (not to be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delated).
perturbation.changed_text: Sutro shall generally avoid making significant changes to the Specifications, processes, Facilities, raw materials, or raw material suppliers affecting Manufacturing activities related to the Product (a "Manufacturing Change") that, to Sutro's reasonable belief, (a) would likely necessitate a change to the applicable Drug Master File, (b) could potentially delay SutroVax in obtaining Regulatory Approval with respect to Vaccine Compositions, or (c) may cause the Product to deviate significantly from the Specification therefor (including the Activity Test with respect to Extract); without seeking SutroVax's input, where feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces several ambiguities that weaken the obligation. 'Shall not make any changes' is replaced with 'shall generally avoid making significant changes,' which is subjective. The phrase 'to Sutro's reasonable belief' makes the assessment of potential impacts on the Drug Master File and Regulatory Approval dependent on Sutro's interpretation. Replacing 'causes the Product to not meet the Specification therefor' with 'may cause the Product to deviate significantly from the Specification therefor' introduces a degree of acceptable deviation. Finally, changing 'prior written consent' to 'seeking SutroVax's input, where feasible' makes the consent process optional and dependent on feasibility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR § 601.12 - Changes to an approved application.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 601.12 mandates that changes to an approved application (including manufacturing changes) must be reported to the FDA. By allowing Sutro to make changes based on its 'reasonable belief' and 'where feasible' without mandatory prior written consent, the modified text could result in Sutro failing to report significant manufacturing changes to the FDA, thus violating the regulatory requirement for change control and reporting. The level of acceptable variation from the required specifications becomes an internal choice, in conflict with the need to strictly adhere to requirements.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities and weakens obligations regarding manufacturing changes, potentially leading to non-compliance with regulatory requirements. The original text mandates that Sutro 'shall not make any changes' without prior written consent, whereas the changed text uses softer language like 'shall generally avoid making significant changes' and 'without seeking SutroVax's input, where feasible,' making the consent process optional. The Law Explanation argues that this could result in Sutro failing to report significant manufacturing changes to the FDA, in violation of 21 CFR § 601.12's change control and reporting mandates. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 'eCFR :: 21 CFR 601.12 -- Changes to an approved application,' suggesting the existence of regulations concerning changes to approved applications, as described in the Law Explanation. Because the modified text permits more discretion and weakens the obligation for reporting and obtaining consent, it creates a legal contradiction.
